food_name: Panzerotti with Mushroom, Arrabbiata Sauce, Cream and Fresh Mozzarella

region_of_origin: Italy (Southern Italian / Apulian origin; commonly served in Singapore)
food_subgroup: Stuffed and fried pasta / dough products

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for panzerotti; 'stuffed pasta food product' is the closest defensible parent category covering filled dough products.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers filled pasta broadly; panzerotti is a fried filled dough product from Southern Italy. This is the closest defensible FoodEx2 parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to panzerotti or this specific Italian stuffed fried dough product.

serving.natural_language_portion: One restaurant serving of panzerotti is approximately 280 g, comprising the fried dough pocket filled with mushrooms, arrabbiata sauce, cream, and fresh mozzarella.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Pasta, cooked, enriched, with added salt (SR Legacy) and component estimates for fried dough, mushroom filling, cream, mozzarella, and arrabbiata sauce
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for panzerotti with this specific filling combination. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries: fried wheat dough (~50% by weight, ~280 kcal/100g), mushrooms sautéed (~10%), tomato arrabbiata sauce (~15%), heavy cream (~10%), and fresh mozzarella (~15%). Weighted composite per 100g calculated from these proportions. Values represent a plausible central estimate; actual restaurant values may vary.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; pasta and fried dough analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for panzerotti with cream and mozzarella. GI estimated at 52 based on analogous fried pasta / filled pasta products from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008, Diabetes Care). Filled pasta (ravioli, tortellini) GI values range 39–56; fried dough products tend toward the upper end of this range. A central estimate of 52 is used. Glycemic load per serving = (52 × 25.8 g carbohydrate per 100g × 2.80 serving factor) / 100 = 37.6 g GL per 280 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~390 mg/100g; ~1092 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish as a single 280 g serving provides approximately 1092 mg sodium, representing nearly 47% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg; portion reduction and requesting reduced-salt preparation are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load per serving (~37.6 GL) and significant refined carbohydrate content from fried wheat dough
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should exercise caution with this dish as the large fried dough portion delivers a high glycemic load of approximately 37.6 per serving, which can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; smaller portions, pairing with non-starchy vegetables, and monitoring blood glucose response are recommended.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat (~6.1 g/100g), high sodium, and deep-fried preparation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish is high in saturated fat from cream and mozzarella and is deep-fried, contributing to elevated LDL cholesterol risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption and consider lower-fat Italian alternatives prepared without frying or heavy cream.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Panzerotti with Mushroom, Arrabbiata Sauce, Cream and Fresh Mozzarella is a Southern Italian fried stuffed dough product, originating from the Apulia (Puglia) region of Italy. The dish consists of a half-moon shaped pocket of leavened wheat dough that is deep-fried until golden and crisp, then filled or topped with sautéed mushrooms, a spicy tomato arrabbiata sauce, fresh heavy cream, and fresh mozzarella cheese. The name 'Pink Spice' is a menu alias used by Pasta Fresca Da Salvatore in Singapore. The exterior is crispy and slightly chewy, while the interior is rich, creamy, and mildly spicy from the arrabbiata sauce. The combination of cream and arrabbiata creates a 'pink sauce' effect, hence the menu name. Nutritionally, this is a calorie-dense dish (~248 kcal/100g) with significant fat content from deep-frying, cream, and mozzarella (13.5 g fat/100g, 6.1 g saturated fat/100g) and moderate protein (8.2 g/100g) from cheese and dough. Sodium is elevated at approximately 390 mg/100g. The glycemic index is estimated at 52 (low GI range) based on analogous filled pasta products, but the glycemic load per full serving is high (~37.6) due to the large dough portion. Clinically, this dish poses concerns for hypertension (high sodium), Type 2 Diabetes (high glycemic load per serving), and cardiovascular disease (saturated fat and deep-frying). It is suitable for vegetarians but not vegans, and contains gluten and dairy allergens.
common_user_queries: Is panzerotti the same as calzone?
What is Pink Spice at Pasta Fresca?
How many calories in panzerotti with cream and mozzarella?
What is the GI of fried pasta?
How much fat is in panzerotti with cream?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific panzerotti variant with cream and mozzarella. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for fried dough, mushrooms, tomato sauce, cream, and mozzarella. GI is estimated from analogous filled pasta products in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component sources used for this composite estimate. All other -1 values in per_100g have been replaced with estimated numeric values from component analysis. The food_name 'Pink Spice' is a menu alias used by Pasta Fresca Da Salvatore (Boat Quay, Singapore) on field survey; the canonical English name used here reflects the actual dish composition: Panzerotti with Mushroom, Arrabbiata Sauce, Cream and Fresh Mozzarella. Panzerotti is a Southern Italian (Apulian) fried stuffed dough product distinct from calzone (which is baked). The arrabbiata sauce combined with cream produces a pink-hued sauce, explaining the menu alias. Although the cuisine is Italian, the dish is served in Singapore and sourced from field survey data, triggering the Singaporean multilingual requirement (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as per PROJECT_VERITAS_Ver2.md section 7.2 for 'Singaporean dishes'. Halal status is unconfirmed as the restaurant has not been verified as halal-certified. Serving size of 280 g is estimated based on typical restaurant panzerotti portions. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI 52 × carbohydrate_g per 100g 25.8 × serving_g 280) / 10000 = 37.6.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Replaced menu alias 'Pink Spice' as food_name with canonical English dish name reflecting actual composition.
Iteration 1: Built weighted component nutrient estimate from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for fried dough, mushrooms, tomato sauce, cream, and mozzarella.
Iteration 1: Documented vitamin_d_mcg as -1 with explicit justification in notes (not reported in component sources).
Iteration 1: Set GI to 52 from analogous filled pasta products (University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008); gi_category correctly set to 'low' (52 ≤ 55).
Iteration 1: Verified all aliases_by_language strings appear verbatim in flat aliases list.
Iteration 1: Confirmed Italian cuisine does not trigger Singaporean multilingual requirement; romanized Italian name provided. (This was incorrect, leading to REJECT).

Iteration 2: Corrected interpretation of multilingual rule: as the dish is served in Singapore and sourced from field survey data, it falls under 'Singaporean dishes' for multilingual requirements. Added Chinese (simplified), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names and aliases.
Iteration 2: Ensured all new local names and aliases are consistently added to both `local_names`, `aliases_by_language`, and the flat `aliases` list.
Iteration 2: Updated `q1_food_identity` and `q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_coverage` notes to reflect corrected multilingual compliance.
Iteration 2: Verified all ontology match_type values use only allowed values (exact, closest_parent, none). Confirmed no invented codes. Final self-score: 8/8 PASS.
